text: I love olives
I really hate cilantro
you said you prefer red wine
Asa enjoys hiking
Donald Trump is the 47th President
Tim Cook is the CEO of Apple
Marie Curie served as professor at the Sorbonne from 1906 to 1934
Trump was inaugurated as the 45th president in 2017
Marie Curie was a physicist
notes: profession-as-category (vs role_assignment); physicist is intrinsic, not conferred

text: the kakapo is a bird
Tokyo is a city
baboons are mammals
GPT-4 is a large language model
there are 50 states in the US
Marie Curie was born in 1867
baboons live about 30 years in the wild
the strawberry has 3 r's in its name
Mount Everest is 8848 meters tall
I live in Williamstown
Tokyo is in Japan
Marie Curie worked at the Sorbonne
Asa visited Boston last week
Williamstown is part of Massachusetts
the engine is part of the car
carbon is part of every organic molecule
the kitchen is part of the house
Trump defeated Kamala Harris in the 2024 election
Anthropic was founded by Dario Amodei
Paris is the capital of France
baboons hunt cooperatively for meat
smoking causes lung cancer
Trump was inaugurated on January 20, 2025
the Berlin Wall fell in 1989
Apple launched the iPhone 15 in September 2023
I believe the earth orbits the sun
she knows that I'm a vegetarian
Asa hopes the project ships next month
Asa lives in Williamstown and Williamstown is part of Massachusetts
notes: FIRST claim only — this is the disambiguation pair; 'lives in' is locational. 2nd claim 'is part of' is mereological.

text: Sundar Pichai is CEO of Google
notes: ambiguous against categorical 'is a CEO'; surface form 'CEO of Google' fixes it as role

text: baboons have a matrilineal social hierarchy
the Mississippi is a river
the Mississippi flows through Minnesota
notes: locational predicate; not mereological (river isn't part of state in the constitutive sense)

text: I think that's a great idea
the sunset was beautiful
what time is it?
let's say I live in Boston for this conversation
notes: session marker present but the claim itself is still extractable

text: Old World monkeys include baboons, macaques, and mandrills.
notes: list-of-examples → ONE relational includes claim, NOT 3 separate categorical is_a claims. The categorical-split would also produce 'baboon is an Old World monkey' which contains the suffix tautology trap.

text: Common examples of lizards include iguanas, geckos, skinks, and monitor lizards.
notes: list-of-examples — relational includes form preferred. categorical-split would generate 'monitor lizard is a lizard' which the validator rejects as a suffix tautology.

text: The squamates include lizards, snakes, and amphisbaenians.
A lizard's diet can be carnivorous, omnivorous, or herbivorous.
notes: range/variation — ONE claim about diet variation, NOT 3 separate 'lizard diet can be X' claims. predicate=diet_varies_among or similar.

text: Lizard sizes range from a few centimeters to over 3 meters.
notes: range/variation — ONE quantitative claim with a range value, NOT separate min/max claims.

text: Lizards play important roles in their ecosystems as both predators and prey.
notes: generic-noun object (ecosystem); abstain. No specific role context to verify.

text: Monkeys exhibit complex social behavior in their environment.
Lizards prey on insects, small mammals, and other reptiles.
notes: same shape as ext-vague-001 BUT object is specific (insects, small mammals, reptiles). Verifier can check this; accept.

text: I love ramen, not sushi
notes: contrastive correction X-not-Y on a SHORT input: gpt-4.1-mini was flattening slot keys to the top level on this exact shape (validator rejected with 'missing field slots'), losing the user's preference silently. v0.14.6 added explicit STRUCTURAL CRITICAL note + worked example. Should yield TWO facts (loves ramen +polarity / loves sushi -polarity).

text: Actually, I love ramen, not sushi
notes: contrastive correction with discourse-marker prefix. The 'Actually,' / 'Wait,' / 'Sorry,' prefix is a CORRECTION cue — extractor must NOT treat it as interrogative-meta. Should yield TWO preference facts.

text: Actually, I love ramen
notes: discourse-marker prefix WITHOUT contrast — extract the new assertion normally; do NOT abstain on the prefix.

text: I prefer ramen to sushi
notes: comparative preference — ONE fact (predicate=prefers, slot=over). Distinct from contrastive corrections, which yield two facts with opposite polarities.

text: I'm a junior at Williams College
notes: v0.14.7 first-person canonicalization for role_assignment: agent must be 'user' (NOT a name). The bug case extracted agent='Asa' from 'My name is Asa and I'm a junior at Williams College' because the prior canonicalization rule was scoped to preference/propositional_attitude only.

text: My name is Asa and I'm a junior at Williams College
notes: v0.14.7 multi-clause: name clause is metadata (no extractable claim — there is no 'name' pattern); role clause extracts ONE role_assignment with agent='user'. MUST NOT also extract a hallucinated 'Williams College located in United States' world fact.

text: I work at Anthropic
notes: v0.14.7 first-person + don't-extract-context-entity-facts: ONE role_assignment with agent='user', org='Anthropic'. Do NOT extract Anthropic.founded_by(...) / Anthropic.is_a(AI company) / Anthropic.located_in(San Francisco) — those are world facts the user did NOT assert.

text: Asa is a junior at Williams College
notes: v0.14.7 — third-person uses the actual name. agent='Asa' is correct here; first-person canonicalization rule only fires on 'I' / 'me' / 'my'.

text: I read Hamlet last week
notes: v0.14.7 — extract ONE event about the user's reading. Do NOT extract Hamlet.authored_by(Shakespeare) or Hamlet.is_a(play) — those are world facts the user did NOT assert.
